claim: High cardiopulmonary fitness causes decreased mortality rate.

result.claim: High cardiopulmonary fitness causes decreased mortality rate.
result.reasoning: The claim that high cardiopulmonary fitness causes decreased mortality rate is strongly supported by the evidence. Paper 5825069, a prospective study of healthy men and women, found that age-adjusted all-cause mortality rates significantly declined with increasing physical fitness, with the most-fit men and women having substantially lower mortality rates compared to the least-fit. This trend remained even after adjusting for various confounding factors. The study concluded that higher physical fitness delays all-cause mortality, primarily due to reduced rates of cardiovascular disease and cancer. Similarly, Paper 12770738, focusing on men with diabetes, demonstrated that a low fitness level was associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease mortality across different weight categories. Furthermore, Paper 438543, a study on women, identified low cardiorespiratory fitness as an important predictor of all-cause mortality, indicating that higher fitness levels are associated with decreased mortality risk. These three papers consistently show a strong inverse relationship between cardiopulmonary fitness and mortality rates in different populations, providing robust evidence for the claim.

evidence.text: Men with low fitness level and overweight had a hazard ratio of 2.7 for mortality.
evidence.source: Cardiorespiratory fitness and body mass index as predictors of cardiovascular disease mortality among men with diabetes.

Design of the Weight-loss Endoscopy Trial (WET): a multi-center, randomized, controlled trial comparing weight loss in endoscopically implanted duodenal-jejunal bypass liners vs. intragastric balloons vs. a sham procedure

evidence.text: Men with low fitness level and class 1 obesity had a hazard ratio of 2.8 for mortality.
evidence.source: Cardiorespiratory fitness and body mass index as predictors of cardiovascular disease mortality among men with diabetes.

evidence.text: The study examined the relation of body mass index (BMI), cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF), and all-cause mortality in women.
evidence.source: The Relation of Body Mass Index, Cardiorespiratory Fitness, and All-Cause Mortality in Women

evidence.text: Men with low fitness level and normal weight had a hazard ratio of 2.7 for mortality.
evidence.source: Cardiorespiratory fitness and body mass index as predictors of cardiovascular disease mortality among men with diabetes.

evidence.text: Increased cardiovascular morbidity and mortality in patients with RA and SLE cannot be entirely explained by traditional risk factors.
evidence.source: Subclinical Atherosclerosis in Rheumatoid Arthritis and Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

evidence.text: Those with decreased CYP2D6 activity had worse disease-free survival (HR, 1.29).
evidence.source: Association between CYP2D6 polymorphisms and outcomes among women with early stage breast cancer treated with tamoxifen.

evidence.text: Age-adjusted all-cause mortality rates declined across physical fitness quintiles from 64.0 per 10,000 person-years in the least-fit men to 18.6 per 10,000 person-years in the most-fit men.
evidence.source: Physical fitness and all-cause mortality. A prospective study of healthy men and women.

evidence.text: Obesity is associated with reduced life expectancy.
evidence.source: EXERCISE IN WEIGHT MANAGEMENT OF OBESITY

evidence.text: Obesity leads to reduced life expectancy.
